title: The Fourth Sunday of Advent
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## December 23, 2018 | Preacher: Broderick Greer

---

LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon was preached on the Fourth Sunday of Advent, the final Sunday before Christmas Day. The Revised Common Lectionary readings for this date would have included 2 Samuel 7:1-11, 16 (David's covenant promise), Psalm 89:1-4, 19-26, Romans 16:25-27, and Luke 1:26-38 (the Annunciation). The congregation was in the final liturgical push of Advent, with Christmas Eve and Christmas Day following within 24-48 hours, creating a heightened anticipatory tone in worship.

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in a period of relative institutional stability following the 2018 General Convention (held in Austin, Texas in July), which had addressed several contentious issues including religious education and polity. The Diocese of Colorado, led by Bishop Robert Wright (elected 2017), was actively engaged in diocesan initiatives and mission work. No major denominational crises or bishop elections were active around this specific date, though the Church continued navigating post-2016 General Convention tensions regarding sexuality and inclusion.

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was in the thick of winter holiday season with winter weather patterns affecting the Rocky Mountain region. The state had experienced significant political engagement earlier in 2018 with the midterm elections (November 6), which resulted in Colorado electing Jared Polis as governor and flipping control of the state legislature. The Denver metro area was focused on holiday preparations and year-end activities in the downtown and Capitol Hill neighborhoods.

NATIONAL NEWS

The partial U.S. federal government shutdown that began December 22, 2018 (over border wall funding disputes) was just beginning and would dominate headlines through January 2019. The Trump administration's family separation policy at the southern border remained a major point of national contention and religious community activism. The #MeToo movement continued shaping national conversations about institutional accountability, with various high-profile cases still unfolding in media and politics.

INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The humanitarian crisis in Venezuela under Nicolás Maduro's regime continued with mass migration and economic collapse drawing international concern. The Syrian civil war remained active with ongoing Russian military support and humanitarian catastrophes, particularly in rebel-held areas. The Brexit negotiations in the UK were in turmoil as Prime Minister Theresa May faced major parliamentary opposition to her withdrawal agreement with the European Union.

CULTURAL MOMENT

The broader American cultural moment was one of deep political polarization and anxiety, amplified by the recent midterm elections and uncertainty about 2020. Christmas 2018 carried an undertone of national division, with the partial shutdown and immigration debates overshadowing traditional holiday sentiment. Denver, as a major metropolitan area with a tech-forward economy and progressive leaning, was simultaneously engaged in holiday celebration and civic concern; the congregation at Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral would have reflected this mixture of hope (the liturgical Advent expectation) and societal worry (the political and social climate).
